error_comment: Task requires drawing and is not text-oriented

question_text: Outline a synthesis of the following compound (N-methyl-4-methoxybenzamide) from the indicated starting material (4-methoxytoluene) and any other reagents. The synthesis pathway shows: 4-methoxytoluene treated with KMnO4 to form 4-methoxybenzoic acid, then treated with SOCl2 to form 4-methoxybenzoyl chloride, followed by reaction with methylamine (NH2CH3) to yield the target amide compound.
canonical_answer: 4-methoxytoluene; KMnO4; 4-methoxybenzoic acid; SOCl2; 4-methoxybenzoyl chloride; NH2CH3; N-methyl-4-methoxybenzamide

question_text: Predict the principal products expected (if any) for the following reaction sequences. For each, select around the structure you want to be evaluated. (2 pts each) A) Reaction of N-methylbenzamide with acetic acid at 25 °C for 10 hrs, yielding no reaction. B) Reaction of cyclohexanecarboxylic acid with SOCl2 at 85 °C followed by 1 equivalent LiAl(OtBu)3 and H2O work-up. C) Reaction of 2 equivalents of phenol with 1 equivalent acetone under acidic catalysis. D) Reaction of 2-methylpentanoic acid-2-d (CO2D) with diazomethane (:N≡N-CH2+ -) in diethyl ether. E) Reaction of a bicyclic anhydride with NaH and EtOH, followed by water work-up.
canonical_answer: A) no reaction; B) cyclohexanecarbaldehyde; C) bisphenol derivative with chiral center; D) methyl ester with CO2CH2D; E) hydroxy ester with CO2Et

question_text:
A) The reaction coordinate diagrams for II and III are meant to represent 'extreme cases.' In the boxes on the right, draw the general functional groups that Loudon is referring to in II and III. (2 pts)

B) Loudon discusses two major factors that could affect the rate of carbonyl substitution. Use these two criteria to compare the rate of reaction of S-ethyl ethanethioate with NaOEt to the reaction of ethyl acetate with NaOEt, whose reaction coordinate diagram is shown in I of Figure 21.6. In the boxes below, explain how each factor would affect the reaction coordinate for the S-ethyl case, including annotated structures to support your arguments. Then sum up your conclusion by completing the statement below the boxes.

Criterion 1: stability of the starting material
These resonance structures require overlap of the sulfur 3p orbital with the carbon 2p orbital, which is not as strong as the overlap of the oxygen 2p orbital with the carbon 2p orbital in ethyl acetate. This loss of resonance stabilization makes S-ethyl ethanethioate less stable than ethyl acetate.

Criterion 2: leaving group ability
The SEt− group is a better leaving group than the OEt− group since the sulfur atom is larger than the oxygen atom and has more diffuse orbitals which can better stabilize the negative charge after the collapse of the tetrahedral intermediate as shown. Alternatively, the pKa of HSEt is lower than the pKa of HOEt, the conjugate base SEt− is a weaker base than OEt−, which makes it a better leaving group.

Circle One: The reaction of S-ethyl ethanethioate with NaOEt will proceed at a FASTER / SLOWER / SAME rate relative to the reaction of ethyl acetate with NaOEt.

C) Based on your arguments in part (b), inscribe an annotated reaction coordinate diagram for the thiol ester reaction in the box labeled IV above. (2 pts)
canonical_answer: A) II: R-C(=O)-NH2; III: R-C(=O)-Cl; B) Criterion 1: The starting material S-ethyl ethanethioate is less stable due to poorer resonance overlap involving sulfur 3p and carbon 2p orbitals compared to oxygen 2p and carbon 2p in ethyl acetate, making the reaction coordinate higher in energy initially. Criterion 2: The SEt− group is a better leaving group than OEt− because sulfur is larger and better stabilizes the negative charge, also due to its lower conjugate acid pKa, making the tetrahedral intermediate collapse easier and lowering energy barrier. Therefore, the reaction of S-ethyl ethanethioate with NaOEt proceeds at a FASTER rate compared to ethyl acetate with NaOEt. C) The reaction coordinate diagram IV should reflect a lower activation barrier due to better leaving group ability, but a higher starting energy due to lower resonance stabilization, showing an annotated curve with these features.

question_text: 5a. Using structures and percentages, describe the sulfur-containing molecular species when ethane thiol (CH3CH2SH) is dissolved in a pH 10.8 buffer solution. The equilibrium between neutral ethane thiol (structure labelled A with SH group) and its conjugate base (structure labelled B with S⁻) is shown with Ka arrow. Relevant calculations using pH - pKa = log([B]/[A]) include 10.8 - 10.6 = log([B]/[A]), 0.2 = log([B]/[A]), [B]/[A] = 1.58, [A] + [B] = 100%, and final values boxed: [A] = 38.8%, [B] = 61.2%. Note: calculator use was not permitted, full credit awarded for correct Henderson–Hasselbalch set-up or other clear demonstrations of how pKa relates to ionization at given pH. 5b. Which of the compounds shown (three structures: a) CH3-CO-O-CH2CH3, b) CH3CH2CH2-O-CHO, c) CH3-CO-O-CH3) fits the following spectral data? Mass Spec: m/z 88.05, IR: 1735 cm⁻¹, 1H NMR: 4.13 ppm (quartet, 2H), 2.21 (singlet, 3H), 0.86 (triplet, 3H). Circle the correct structure. The 1H NMR spectrum with three peaks labelled a, b, c corresponding to the protons on the compounds is shown with ppm scale 5 to 0.
